ucs.title: [LLM] Silk Typhoon (HAFNIUM) Exchange w3wp.exe spawning interpreters/recon — ProxyLogon-style web shell post-exploitation
ucs.description: Hunts the canonical Silk Typhoon / HAFNIUM tradecraft of exploiting on-prem Exchange (ProxyLogon CVE-2021-26855 chain and successors) and using the IIS Exchange app-pool worker process w3wp.exe to spawn cmd/PowerShell, recon LOLBins, or procdump as a child of the dropped China-Chopper-style .aspx web shell. The parent w3wp.exe with an Exchange app-pool command line spawning these binaries is essentially never benign.
ucs.splunk: | tstats `security_content_summariesonly` count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name=w3wp.exe Processes.process_name IN ("cmd.exe","powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","mshta.exe","cscript.exe","wscript.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","whoami.exe","systeminfo.exe","ipconfig.exe","tasklist.exe","quser.exe","procdump.exe","procdump64.exe","rundll32.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe") by Processes.dest Processes.user Processes.parent_process Processes.parent_process_id Processes.process Processes.process_name Processes.process_id Processes.process_hash | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | where like(parent_process,"%MSExchange%AppPool%") OR like(parent_process,"%w3wp.exe -ap %Exchange%") | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
ucs.kql:
// Silk Typhoon (HAFNIUM) — Exchange w3wp post-exploit child process
let ExchangeAppPools = dynamic(["MSExchangeOWAAppPool","MSExchangeECPAppPool","MSExchangeAutodiscoverAppPool","MSExchangeServicesAppPool","MSExchangeRpcProxyAppPool","MSExchangeRestAppPool","MSExchangeMapiFrontEndAppPool","MSExchangeMapiMailboxAppPool","MSExchangePowerShellAppPool","MSExchangePowerShellFrontEndAppPool"]);
let HafniumChildBins = dynamic(["cmd.exe","powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","mshta.exe","cscript.exe","wscript.exe","net.exe","net1.exe","whoami.exe","systeminfo.exe","ipconfig.exe","tasklist.exe","quser.exe","nltest.exe","procdump.exe","procdump64.exe","rundll32.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe"]);
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(30d)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "w3wp.exe"
| where InitiatingProcessCommandLine has_any (ExchangeAppPools)
| where FileName in~ (HafniumChildBins)
| where AccountName !endswith "$"   // exclude machine-account scheduled work
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName,
          ParentCmd = InitiatingProcessCommandLine,
          Child = FileName, ChildCmd = ProcessCommandLine,
          ChildSHA256 = SHA256, FolderPath
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.sentinel_kql:
// Silk Typhoon (HAFNIUM) — Exchange w3wp post-exploit child process (Sentinel SecurityEvent 4688)
let ExchangeAppPools = dynamic(["MSExchangeOWAAppPool","MSExchangeECPAppPool","MSExchangeAutodiscoverAppPool","MSExchangeServicesAppPool","MSExchangeRpcProxyAppPool","MSExchangeRestAppPool","MSExchangeMapiFrontEndAppPool","MSExchangeMapiMailboxAppPool","MSExchangePowerShellAppPool","MSExchangePowerShellFrontEndAppPool"]);
let HafniumChildPaths = dynamic(["\\cmd.exe","\\powershell.exe","\\pwsh.exe","\\mshta.exe","\\cscript.exe","\\wscript.exe","\\net.exe","\\net1.exe","\\whoami.exe","\\systeminfo.exe","\\ipconfig.exe","\\tasklist.exe","\\quser.exe","\\nltest.exe","\\procdump.exe","\\procdump64.exe","\\rundll32.exe","\\certutil.exe","\\bitsadmin.exe"]);
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where EventID == 4688
| where ParentProcessName endswith "\\w3wp.exe"
| where NewProcessName has_any (HafniumChildPaths)
| where Account !endswith "$"
| extend AppPoolHint = extract(@"-ap\s+\"?([^\"\s]+)\"?", 1, CommandLine)
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Account, ParentProcessName,
          NewProcessName, CommandLine, TokenElevationType, AppPoolHint
| order by TimeGenerated desc
ucs.sigma_yaml:
title: Silk Typhoon (HAFNIUM) Exchange Web Shell Post-Exploitation Child Process
id: 6f5d9c1e-3e6d-4b6a-9d5b-1a3a3c1f8d2c
status: experimental
description: |
  Detects w3wp.exe (Exchange IIS app pool) spawning interpreters, recon LOLBins, or credential-dumping tooling — the canonical post-exploitation pattern of Silk Typhoon / HAFNIUM (G0125) following ProxyLogon-class Exchange exploitation and China-Chopper-style .aspx web shell drops.
references:
  - https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2021/03/02/hafnium-targeting-exchange-servers/
  - https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2025/03/05/silk-typhoon-targeting-it-supply-chain/
  - https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0125/
author: SOC Detection Engineering
date: 2026/05/10
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection_parent:
    ParentImage|endswith: '\w3wp.exe'
  selection_child:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\cmd.exe'
      - '\powershell.exe'
      - '\pwsh.exe'
      - '\mshta.exe'
      - '\cscript.exe'
      - '\wscript.exe'
      - '\net.exe'
      - '\net1.exe'
      - '\whoami.exe'
      - '\systeminfo.exe'
      - '\ipconfig.exe'
      - '\tasklist.exe'
      - '\quser.exe'
      - '\nltest.exe'
      - '\procdump.exe'
      - '\procdump64.exe'
      - '\rundll32.exe'
      - '\certutil.exe'
      - '\bitsadmin.exe'
  filter_user:
    User|endswith: '$'
  condition: selection_parent and selection_child and not filter_user
fields:
  - ComputerName
  - User
  - Image
  - CommandLine
  - ParentImage
  - ParentCommandLine
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate Exchange administration scripts run inside an OWA/ECP request context (rare; should be allow-listed by hash + admin account)
level: critical
tags:
  - attack.initial_access
  - attack.t1190
  - attack.persistence
  - attack.t1505.003
  - attack.execution
  - attack.t1059.001
  - attack.t1059.003
  - attack.g0125
ucs.rationale: Silk Typhoon / HAFNIUM (G0125, Operation Exchange Marauder) is the named actor that mass-exploited on-prem Exchange via ProxyLogon (CVE-2021-26855/26857/26858/27065) in early 2021 and continues to chase Exchange/IT-edge bugs through 2024–2026. Their distinctive post-exploitation pattern, documented by MSTIC/DART, is to drop China-Chopper / Antsword .aspx shells under Exchange virtual directories a

ucs.title: [LLM] Silk Typhoon (HAFNIUM) Tarrask hidden scheduled-task persistence — TaskCache\Tree SD value deletion
ucs.description: Detects the Tarrask malware persistence trick — attributed by Microsoft directly to HAFNIUM — where the actor creates a Windows scheduled task and then deletes the task's Security Descriptor (`SD`) registry value under `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree\<TaskName>\SD`. With the SD value missing, the task is invisible to schtasks.exe, the Task Scheduler MMC, and `schtasks /query`, but still executes — a near-unique HAFNIUM defence-evasion signature.
ucs.splunk: | tstats `security_content_summariesonly` count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime from datamodel=Endpoint.Registry where Registry.action=deleted Registry.registry_path="*\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Schedule\\TaskCache\\Tree\\*" Registry.registry_value_name=SD by Registry.dest Registry.user Registry.process_guid Registry.process_id Registry.registry_path Registry.registry_value_name | `drop_dm_object_name(Registry)` | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)` | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
ucs.kql:
// Silk Typhoon (HAFNIUM) — Tarrask hidden scheduled task: SD value deleted from TaskCache\Tree
DeviceRegistryEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(30d)
| where ActionType in ("RegistryValueDeleted","RegistryKeyDeleted")
| where RegistryKey has @"\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree\"
| where RegistryValueName =~ "SD"
   or RegistryKey endswith @"\SD"
| where InitiatingProcessFileName !in~ ("trustedinstaller.exe","msiexec.exe")  // legit servicing rarely touches this
| project Timestamp, DeviceName,
          TaskTreePath = RegistryKey,
          ValueDeleted = RegistryValueName,
          Actor = InitiatingProcessAccountName,
          ActorProcess = InitiatingProcessFileName,
          ActorCmd = InitiatingProcessCommandLine,
          ActorParent = InitiatingProcessParentFileName,
          ActorIntegrity = InitiatingProcessIntegrityLevel
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.sentinel_kql:
// Silk Typhoon (HAFNIUM) — Tarrask hidden scheduled task: SD value deleted from TaskCache\Tree (ASIM)
ImRegistryEvent
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where EventType in ("RegistryValueDeleted","RegistryKeyDeleted","DeleteValue")
| where RegistryKey contains @"\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree\"
| where RegistryValue =~ "SD"
   or RegistryKey endswith @"\SD"
| where ActingProcessName !endswith "\\trustedinstaller.exe"
   and ActingProcessName !endswith "\\msiexec.exe"
| project TimeGenerated, DvcHostname,
          TaskTreePath = RegistryKey,
          ValueDeleted = RegistryValue,
          Actor = ActorUsername,
          ActorProcess = ActingProcessName,
          ActorCmd = ActingProcessCommandLine
| order by TimeGenerated desc
ucs.sigma_yaml:
title: Silk Typhoon (HAFNIUM) Tarrask Hidden Scheduled Task SD-Value Deletion
id: 8d4a5b21-7c1e-4f3b-9b22-a7c1e9d4c2f1
status: experimental
description: |
  Detects deletion of the SD (security descriptor) value beneath HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree\<TaskName> — the signature persistence-hiding trick used by the Tarrask malware, attributed by Microsoft to HAFNIUM / Silk Typhoon (G0125). With this value gone the scheduled task continues to execute but disappears from schtasks.exe and the Task Scheduler GUI.
references:
  - https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2022/04/12/tarrask-malware-uses-scheduled-tasks-for-defense-evasion/
  - https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0125/
  - https://attack.mitre.org/software/S0987/
author: SOC Detection Engineering
date: 2026/05/10
logsource:
  category: registry_delete
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetObject|contains: '\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree\'
    TargetObject|endswith: '\SD'
    EventType: 'DeleteValue'
  filter_servicing:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\TrustedInstaller.exe'
      - '\msiexec.exe'
  condition: selection and not filter_servicing
fields:
  - ComputerName
  - User
  - Image
  - TargetObject
  - EventType
falsepositives:
  - Extremely rare — Windows itself does not delete the SD value for a scheduled task during normal lifecycle operations; legitimate task removal deletes the entire Tree subkey, not just the SD value.
level: critical
tags:
  - attack.persistence
  - attack.defense_evasion
  - attack.t1053.005
  - attack.t1562.001
  - attack.t1070.009
  - attack.g0125
  - attack.s0987
ucs.rationale: The Tarrask malware (S0987) is, per Microsoft Threat Intelligence Center's April 2022 blog, attributed specifically to HAFNIUM / Silk Typhoon (G0125). Tarrask's distinguishing tradecraft is not creating a scheduled task — many actors do that — but selectively deleting the SD value under the task's TaskCache\Tree key, which exploits a quirk where the Task Scheduler API treats a missing SD as 'no pe
